nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes with strong currents; commonly perches on large gorgonians and black-coral colonies.

decorPreferences: structured rockwork with crevices and perching ledges
gorgonian or black-coral analogues for natural perching

diet.notes: Feeds on small benthic and planktonic crustaceans in the wild. Generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of frozen mysis, enriched brine, chopped marine meats and the occasional live feeder shrimp.

goodWith: medium to large semi-aggressive reef fish
avoidWith: small cleaner shrimp and sexy shrimp

summary: The Longnose Hawkfish (Oxycirrhites typus) is a reef perching predator recognized by its distinctive elongated snout. Generally considered reef-safe with caution, it tends to prey on ornamental shrimp and other small motile invertebrates.
careNotes: The Longnose Hawkfish is a perch-oriented ambush predator with a distinctive elongated snout adapted for plucking small crustaceans from crevices. In the wild it is most often observed clinging to large gorgonian sea fans and black-coral colonies on steep outer reef slopes, often well below 25 m. Aquarium analogues of these perching structures (open rockwork with elevated ledges, faux gorgonians, or live gorgonian/black-coral colonies where appropriate) are generally advised. Lacks a swim bladder, so it spends most of its time perched rather than cruising. Generally considered reef-safe with caution: its long snout and small mouth let it leave most corals alone, but it tends to hunt ornamental shrimp, small crabs, and other small motile invertebrates, and has been reported to harass tiny fish such as gobies and dartfish. Highly territorial toward conspecifics and other hawkfishes; one per tank is the usual rule unless a confirmed mated pair is introduced together. Has been reported to form monogamous pairs in the wild and is the only hawkfish species commonly reported to have spawned in captivity. A tight-fitting lid is recommended as the species can jump when startled.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity more often than other hawkfishes. Wild spawning occurs at dusk as a rapid paired ascent of less than a metre, after which the pair returns to their resident coral. Rearing the pelagic larvae through metamorphosis is generally considered very difficult and has been only rarely accomplished by hobbyists.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cirrhitidae, Centrarchiformes), 13 cm TL max length, Indo-Pacific + Eastern Pacific distribution, 10-100 m depth range, 24.9-29 C wild temperature range, and monogamous spawning behaviour.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Used as third source after disagreement on tank size (LiveAquaria 30 gal vs TFH 60-100 gal) and care level (LiveAquaria 'easy' vs TFH 'moderate-to-advanced'); chose intermediate and 200 L as compromise values. Confirmed monogamous pairing, high conspecific aggression, lack of swim bladder and gorgonian/black-coral perching habit.

fish.breedingNotes: The Longnose Hawkfish spawns as a monogamous pair, releasing pelagic eggs during a brief paired ascent. Rearing the larvae through metamorphosis is generally considered very difficult and has only rarely been accomplished by hobbyists.
